questions.question: A retail company's point-of-sale system suddenly starts calculating incorrect tax amounts after a routine database update. The development team needs to restore correct functionality. Which type of maintenance is required?
options: Adaptive maintenance -- modifying software to meet changing business requirements
Corrective maintenance -- fixing a defect to restore correct functioning
Perfective maintenance -- improving the system's performance
Preventive maintenance -- proactively identifying potential problems
questions.rationale: Corrective maintenance is the process of fixing defects or bugs in the software. The goal is to restore the software to its correct functioning state. The incorrect tax calculation is a defect introduced by the update that must be diagnosed and fixed.

questions.question: A healthcare management system was originally built for a single country. The company now expands to Southeast Asia and needs the system to support multiple languages, local regulatory requirements, and different date formats. Which maintenance type applies?
options: Emergency maintenance -- fixing critical issues as quickly as possible
Perfective maintenance -- enhancing the software's capabilities
Corrective maintenance -- fixing software bugs and defects
Adaptive maintenance -- modifying software to meet changing requirements
questions.rationale: Adaptive maintenance is the process of modifying software to meet changing user or business requirements. Expanding to new regions with different languages, regulations, and date formats represents changing business needs. The world changes, so the software must adapt.

questions.question: An e-commerce platform's search feature works correctly but takes 8 seconds to return results, causing user frustration. The team decides to add caching and optimize database queries to reduce response time to under 1 second. Which maintenance type is this?
options: Corrective maintenance -- the search has a functional bug
Preventive maintenance -- preventing future performance issues
Perfective maintenance -- improving software performance and quality
Emergency maintenance -- resolving a critical user-facing issue
questions.rationale: Perfective maintenance improves the software's functionality or performance. The search feature works correctly (no bug), but is slow. Optimizing speed through caching and query optimization is an enhancement to quality and performance, not a defect fix.

questions.question: A fintech startup schedules quarterly penetration testing and code security audits for their banking application, even though no security incidents have occurred. Which maintenance type best describes this proactive activity?
options: Corrective maintenance
Adaptive maintenance
Emergency maintenance
Preventive maintenance
questions.rationale: Preventive maintenance proactively identifies and fixes potential problems before they become actual problems. Its goal is to reduce the risk of software failure. Regular security audits and penetration testing -- conducted before any incidents occur -- are textbook preventive measures, including implementing security best practices such as encryption, access controls, and firewalls.

questions.question: A national airline's booking system crashes during peak holiday season, causing thousands of customers to be unable to purchase tickets. Flights are being delayed and the company is losing revenue by the minute. Which maintenance type is required?
options: Perfective maintenance -- improving system reliability
Preventive maintenance -- should have been done earlier
Corrective maintenance -- diagnosing and fixing the root cause
Emergency maintenance -- restoring service as quickly as possible
questions.rationale: Emergency maintenance is the process of fixing critical issues as quickly as possible. Its goal is to minimize downtime and ensure the software remains functional. A crashed booking system during peak season requires immediate attention -- the team may need to roll back changes, implement a temporary fix, and restore the system to working order urgently.

questions.question: A developer notices their team's legacy codebase has become increasingly difficult to navigate. They propose spending two sprints refactoring the code, improving variable naming, and restructuring modules -- without changing any user-facing functionality. Which maintenance type does this represent?
options: Corrective maintenance -- fixing defects in the code
Adaptive maintenance -- adapting to new requirements
Perfective maintenance -- improving software quality and maintainability
Emergency maintenance -- addressing urgent technical debt
questions.rationale: Perfective maintenance enhances the software's capabilities and improves its quality. Refactoring code for better maintainability -- optimizing code organization, improving naming, and restructuring modules -- is an improvement to internal quality. The lecture notes list 'refactoring code, optimizing performance, and improving maintainability' as key maintenance activities for codebases.

questions.question: In a software team, a user reports that the application's dashboard displays charts incorrectly on mobile devices. The user has no access to the codebase. According to the responsibility framework for fixing issues, who should fix this problem?
options: The user, since they discovered and are experiencing the problem
The IT technician, since it involves display/hardware rendering
The developer or development team, since it is a software defect
The system administrator, since they have server access
questions.rationale: According to the responsibility framework, who fixes the problem depends on the nature of the issue. Software bugs/defects are the responsibility of the developer or development team. Incorrect chart rendering is a software defect. The user's role is to report the problem; an IT technician handles hardware issues, not application-level rendering bugs.

questions.question: A junior developer writes a README file for their project that includes a line-by-line explanation of every function in the source code, spanning over 30 pages. Which README guideline does this primarily violate?
options: "Provide examples" -- they should include only code snippets instead
"Use clear language" -- technical jargon should be avoided entirely
"Keep it concise" and "Do not explain entire source code"
"Update regularly" -- such a long README will quickly become outdated
questions.rationale: Good README guidelines state: keep it concise (avoid lengthy descriptions), and it is unnecessary to include details about how the source code is programmed. The README's purpose is to provide information about the project and how to use it, NOT a detailed technical explanation of the code. A line-by-line explanation of every function directly violates both conciseness and the rule against explaining the entire source code.

questions.question: A software team categorizes their maintenance work using the CAPPE framework. A new task arrives: a government regulation now requires all user data to be stored within the country's borders, forcing a database migration. Under which CAPPE category does this task fall?
options: Corrective -- the current storage violates a rule
Adaptive -- the software must adapt to a changed external requirement
Preventive -- proactively preventing legal penalties
Perfective -- improving the data architecture
questions.rationale: CAPPE stands for Corrective, Adaptive, Perfective, Preventive, and Emergency. Adaptive maintenance modifies the software to meet changing user or business requirements. A new government regulation is an external change that forces the software to adapt. This is not corrective (no bug), not preventive (not proactive -- the requirement already exists), and not perfective (not an optional improvement).

questions.question: A project's README includes sections for Project Overview, Installation Guide, Usage Examples, and a Troubleshooting FAQ -- but users have no way to seek help for issues not covered in the FAQ. According to README best practices, what critical element is missing?
options: A detailed changelog listing every version and its changes
Contact information or a link to a support forum
A complete API reference with every endpoint documented
A list of all contributors with their roles and responsibilities
questions.rationale: Good README guidelines specify that you should provide contact information such as an email address or link to a support forum where users can get help or report issues. Without this, users have no escalation path for problems not already addressed in the documentation.

questions.question: A hospital's patient records system correctly stores all data, but a new national healthcare regulation requires every patient record to include a unique national health ID that was not previously tracked. Separately, appointment reminders are being sent at the wrong time due to a timezone bug. Which maintenance types are needed for each issue, respectively?
options: Emergency and Corrective
Adaptive and Corrective
Perfective and Adaptive
Corrective and Preventive
questions.rationale: The new regulation is an external requirement change requiring adaptive maintenance (modifying software to meet changing requirements -- 'world changes = software must adapt'). The timezone bug causing incorrect reminders is a defect requiring corrective maintenance (fixing bugs to restore correct functioning). Each issue maps to a different CAPPE type based on its nature.

questions.question: A development team is planning their next sprint. Task A is to add multi-factor authentication because the security team wants to proactively reduce the risk of future breaches (none have occurred). Task B is to fix a login error that prevents 2% of users from signing in. What are the CAPPE types for Task A and Task B, respectively?
options: Adaptive and Emergency
Preventive and Corrective
Perfective and Emergency
Corrective and Preventive
questions.rationale: Task A is preventive maintenance -- proactively identifying and fixing potential problems (security breaches) before they occur. No incident has happened; the team acts to reduce future risk. Task B is corrective maintenance -- fixing a defect (login error) that currently affects users to restore correct functioning.

questions.question: A company's CRM application works perfectly, but the CEO requests the dashboard be redesigned with a more modern look and improved data visualizations. No users have complained, and no external requirement has changed. Which maintenance type applies?
options: Corrective -- fixing a visual defect in the dashboard
Adaptive -- meeting a changed business requirement from the CEO
Perfective -- improving the software's usability and quality
Emergency -- the CEO's request should be treated as urgent
questions.rationale: Perfective maintenance improves the software's functionality, performance, or usability, or adds new features. The dashboard works correctly and no external requirement has changed. The CEO is requesting a quality improvement (better usability/aesthetics), which is an enhancement to the software's capabilities, not a bug fix, external adaptation, or emergency.

questions.question: A system administrator reports that the company's internal tool is running slowly. Investigation reveals the slowdown is caused by a hardware limitation -- the server's RAM is at 95% capacity -- not a software defect. According to the responsibility framework, who should address this?
options: The developer/development team -- they should optimize the code
The user -- they should use the tool differently
The IT technician -- the issue is hardware-related
The system administrator -- they reported it, so they fix it
questions.rationale: According to the responsibility framework, the nature of the issue determines who fixes it. Hardware problems are the IT technician's responsibility. Even though a system administrator reported the issue, the root cause is hardware (insufficient RAM), not a software bug (developer) or user behaviour error (user).

questions.question: A software team discovers that the third-party payment API their application depends on has announced deprecation in 6 months, with a new version already available. The team begins migrating to the new API version immediately. Which maintenance type is this?
options: Preventive -- proactively addressing the issue before it causes a failure
Adaptive -- the software must be modified to meet a changed external requirement
Corrective -- the current API integration will eventually break
Perfective -- migrating to a newer API improves quality
questions.rationale: Adaptive maintenance modifies software to meet changing requirements. The external world has changed (the API is being deprecated and replaced). 'World changes = software must adapt.' While the proactive timing might suggest preventive, the root cause is an external requirement change (new API version), making this adaptive. Preventive maintenance applies when there is no known external trigger -- e.g., conducting security audits proactively.

questions.question: Consider two scenarios. In Scenario A, a team optimizes database queries to reduce page load times from 5 seconds to 1 second. In Scenario B, a team adds support for a new currency after their company expands to Japan. These are examples of which maintenance types, respectively?
options: Corrective and Adaptive
Perfective and Adaptive
Preventive and Perfective
Adaptive and Corrective
questions.rationale: Scenario A is perfective maintenance -- improving the software's performance (speed optimization) without fixing a defect. Scenario B is adaptive maintenance -- modifying the software to meet a changing business requirement (expansion to a new market). The lecture notes give a near-identical adaptive example: 'add support for different currencies and exchange rates' when a company expands.

questions.question: A user contacts support saying they accidentally sent an email to the wrong recipient because they selected the wrong contact from a dropdown list. The software sent the email exactly as instructed. According to the responsibility framework, who is responsible for this error?
options: The developer -- the dropdown should prevent wrong selections
The IT technician -- it involves the email system configuration
The user -- this is a user behaviour error
The system administrator -- they should restrict email capabilities
questions.rationale: This is a user (behaviour) error. The software performed exactly as intended -- the user selected a contact and the email was sent to that contact. The responsibility framework distinguishes: software bugs/defects are the developer's responsibility, user behaviour errors are the user's responsibility, and hardware issues are the IT technician's responsibility.

questions.question: A README file contains: (1) a project overview, (2) step-by-step installation instructions with prerequisites, (3) a complete explanation of every class and function in the source code, and (4) contact information for support. Which element violates the README guidelines?
options: The project overview -- it should be more detailed
The installation instructions -- prerequisites should be in a separate file
The source code explanation -- READMEs should not explain entire source code
The contact information -- only a repository link is needed
questions.rationale: README guidelines state it is unnecessary to include details about how the source code is programmed. The purpose is to provide information about the project and how to use it, NOT a detailed technical explanation of the code. 'DO NOT explain entire source code.' The other elements (overview, installation with prerequisites, contact info) are all recommended README components.

questions.question: According to README best practices, the README should use language appropriate for which audience?
options: Only experienced developers who will contribute to the codebase
Both technical and non-technical users
Only the project's original development team
Only system administrators responsible for deployment
questions.rationale: README guidelines emphasize using 'simple and clear language that is easy to understand, even for non-technical users' and to 'avoid using technical jargon. Not everyone is a developer.' This indicates the README should be accessible to a broad audience, not just technical contributors.

questions.question: A company's web application has been running smoothly for 2 years. The development team schedules monthly activities including backing up databases, monitoring server logs for anomalies, and reviewing access control policies. No specific problems have been identified. Which maintenance type is being performed?
options: Corrective -- they are looking for bugs to fix
Adaptive -- they are preparing for future changes
Perfective -- they are improving system quality
Preventive -- they are proactively reducing the risk of failure
questions.rationale: Preventive maintenance proactively identifies and fixes potential problems before they become actual problems. Its goal is to reduce the risk of software failure and ensure reliability. The lecture notes specifically list 'performing regular backups, monitoring system logs, or conducting security audits' as examples of preventive maintenance activities.

questions.question: An airline's booking system crashes during a holiday rush. The team performs emergency maintenance, deploying a temporary fix to restore service. Later, they conduct a thorough root-cause analysis and implement a permanent fix. What type of maintenance is the permanent fix?
options: Another round of emergency maintenance
Corrective maintenance
Preventive maintenance
Perfective maintenance
questions.rationale: Emergency maintenance focuses on restoring service as quickly as possible, often using temporary fixes or rollbacks. Once the crisis is over, fixing the underlying defect through proper diagnosis and a permanent solution is corrective maintenance -- restoring the software to its correct functioning state. The urgency that characterized the emergency phase is no longer present.

questions.question: A software product currently runs only on Windows. Market research shows that 40% of potential customers use macOS, so the CEO decides the team should build macOS compatibility. Which maintenance type is this?
options: Perfective -- improving the product's reach and features
Corrective -- the lack of macOS support is a defect
Adaptive -- modifying software to meet changing market requirements
Preventive -- preventing loss of potential customers
questions.rationale: Adaptive maintenance modifies software to meet changing user or business requirements. The market requirement has changed -- the company now needs to serve macOS users. This parallels the lecture example of expanding to new regions requiring new language and currency support. The software is not broken (not corrective), is not being optimized (not perfective), and the change is driven by a specific external need (not proactive prevention).

questions.question: Both corrective and emergency maintenance involve fixing problems. According to the lecture notes, what is the PRIMARY distinction between them?
options: Corrective fixes bugs while emergency adds new features
Corrective addresses defects through a standard process, while emergency fixes critical issues requiring immediate attention to minimize downtime
Corrective is done by developers while emergency is done by IT technicians
Corrective is for software issues while emergency is for hardware failures
questions.rationale: Both types involve fixing problems, but the key distinction is severity and urgency. Corrective maintenance restores software to its correct functioning state through a standard process (identify, diagnose, develop fix, deploy). Emergency maintenance fixes critical issues 'as quickly as possible' with the goal of minimizing downtime -- it may involve rolling back changes or implementing temporary fixes to restore the system to working order immediately.

questions.question: A developer writes a README that says: 'Installation: Run pip install myapp'. There are no mentions of prerequisites, required Python version, OS compatibility, or configuration steps. Which README guideline is being violated?
options: "Keep it concise" -- the installation section is too short
"Explain installation and usage" -- include prerequisites, setup, and configuration details
"Provide examples" -- code examples are missing
"Provide contact information" -- there is no support link
questions.rationale: The README guidelines state that installation and usage instructions should 'include any prerequisites, set up instructions, or configuration details that may be required.' Simply saying 'pip install myapp' without mentioning required Python version, OS compatibility, dependencies, or configuration violates this guideline. Conciseness is about avoiding unnecessary detail, not omitting essential information.

questions.question: What is the fundamental goal shared by ALL five maintenance types in the CAPPE framework?
options: To fix bugs and restore correct functionality
To ensure software remains reliable, functional, and relevant over time
To add new features and improve performance
To reduce the cost of software development
questions.rationale: While each CAPPE type has its own specific goal (corrective: restore functioning, adaptive: keep relevant, perfective: enhance capabilities, preventive: reduce failure risk, emergency: minimize downtime), they all share the overarching purpose of ensuring 'software remains reliable, functional, and relevant over time by focusing on specific goals and objectives.' This helps developers prioritize work and develop effective maintenance strategies.

questions.question: A company's inventory management system has separate modules for each warehouse. As the company expands internationally, the team must modify the database schema to accommodate new warehouses and locations, add multi-language support, and handle different currencies. According to the lecture notes, which maintenance type does this DIRECTLY match?
options: Corrective maintenance -- the database cannot handle new data
Adaptive maintenance -- adapting to business expansion
Perfective maintenance -- improving database efficiency
Preventive maintenance -- preparing for future growth
questions.rationale: This directly mirrors the lecture example for adaptive maintenance: 'company has developed a web-based inventory management system... company expands and has warehouses in different countries... make changes to the UI to support multiple languages, add support for different currencies and exchange rates, modify DB schema to accommodate new warehouses and locations.' Adaptive maintenance modifies software to meet changing business requirements.

questions.question: During an emergency maintenance response to a system crash, which of the following actions is described in the lecture notes as a typical step?
options: Adding new features to prevent future crashes
Conducting penetration testing on the system
Rolling back changes that may have caused the problem
Refactoring the codebase for better maintainability
questions.rationale: The lecture notes specifically describe emergency maintenance steps as: identifying the root cause of the error, rolling back changes that may have caused the problem, and implementing a temporary fix to restore the system to working order. Adding features is perfective, penetration testing is preventive, and refactoring is perfective.

questions.question: A project README states: 'This application uses a proprietary recursive algorithm with O(n log n) complexity implementing a modified Dijkstra-based pathfinding approach with dynamic programming optimization.' This description primarily violates which README guideline?
options: "Keep it concise" -- the description is too long
"Use clear language" -- avoid technical jargon; not everyone is a developer
"Provide examples" -- it should show the algorithm in action
"Update regularly" -- algorithms change over time
questions.rationale: The README guidelines emphasize using 'simple and clear language that is easy to understand, even for non-technical users' and to 'avoid using technical jargon. Not everyone is a developer.' Terms like 'recursive algorithm,' 'O(n log n) complexity,' and 'Dijkstra-based pathfinding' are highly technical jargon that would be incomprehensible to non-technical users.

questions.question: According to the responsibility framework, which TWO factors (besides the nature of the issue) determine who is responsible for fixing a software problem?
options: Cost of the fix and timeline for delivery
Role and level of authority
Severity of the bug and number of users affected
Programming language used and deployment environment
questions.rationale: The lecture notes identify three factors: (1) Role -- developers fix software bugs, users report problems; (2) Level of authority -- only someone with access to the system/code can make changes (e.g., having admin access or codebase access); (3) Nature of the issue -- software bugs go to developers, user errors to users, hardware to IT technicians.

questions.question: Which of the following scenarios represents the CORRECT pairing of issue type and responsible party according to the responsibility matrix?
options: A network outage --> Developer/development team
A software crash when clicking 'Submit' --> IT technician
A user enters their password incorrectly --> User
A database query returns wrong results --> System administrator
questions.rationale: According to the responsibility matrix: software bugs/defects --> developer/development team, user (behaviour) errors --> user, hardware issues --> IT technician. A user entering their password incorrectly is a user behaviour error. A network outage would be hardware/infrastructure (IT technician), a software crash is a software defect (developer), and wrong query results are a software defect (developer).

questions.question: A software application for managing customer orders is becoming slow and unresponsive as volume grows. According to the lecture notes' specific example of perfective maintenance, which action is NOT listed as a typical response?
options: Optimizing the code to improve performance
Refactoring the database schema to improve efficiency
Adding caching mechanisms to reduce server load
Implementing multi-factor authentication for security
questions.rationale: The lecture notes give a specific perfective maintenance example for this exact scenario, listing three actions: optimizing code to improve performance, refactoring the database schema to improve efficiency, and adding caching mechanisms to reduce server load. Implementing multi-factor authentication is a security measure that falls under preventive maintenance (reducing risk of breaches), not perfective.

questions.question: A README file has not been updated in 18 months, even though the software has undergone three major releases with significant feature changes and two API redesigns. What risk does this create according to README guidelines?
options: The README becomes too long for users to read
Users may encounter confusion and errors due to outdated information
The software will stop functioning without an updated README
Developers will lose access to the codebase
questions.rationale: The README guidelines state you should 'keep README up to date with any changes to the software or its documentation.' This 'helps users stay informed and reduces the risk of confusion or errors.' An outdated README means installation instructions, usage examples, and API documentation may no longer be accurate, leading users astray.

questions.question: A team receives four maintenance requests: (1) the payment system is completely down during business hours, (2) users would like a dark-mode feature, (3) a quarterly security audit is scheduled, (4) error messages display a minor typo. What are the correct CAPPE classifications for each?
options: 1-Emergency, 2-Adaptive, 3-Corrective, 4-Perfective
1-Corrective, 2-Perfective, 3-Preventive, 4-Corrective
1-Emergency, 2-Perfective, 3-Preventive, 4-Corrective
1-Emergency, 2-Adaptive, 3-Preventive, 4-Perfective
questions.rationale: (1) Payment system down during business hours is a critical failure requiring immediate attention -- Emergency. (2) Adding dark mode is a usability improvement with no external requirement change -- Perfective. (3) Scheduled security audits proactively identify potential problems -- Preventive. (4) A typo in error messages is a defect to be fixed -- Corrective. Understanding these classifications helps developers prioritize work.

questions.question: According to the lecture notes, what is the relationship between software updates/upgrades and maintenance?
options: Updates and upgrades replace the need for maintenance entirely
Maintenance is required to implement updates and upgrades to keep software current
Updates are corrective maintenance; upgrades are always adaptive maintenance
Maintenance only begins after all updates and upgrades are complete
questions.rationale: The lecture notes state that 'software evolves over time, updates and upgrades are often required to add new features, improve performance, or address security vulnerabilities' and that 'maintenance is required to implement these updates and upgrades to ensure that the software remains up-to-date and can continue to meet needs of its users.' Updates/upgrades are part of the ongoing maintenance process.

questions.question: A development team categorizes all their maintenance work using CAPPE. Which statement best explains why this classification approach is valuable?
options: It eliminates the need for software documentation
It helps developers prioritize work and develop effective maintenance strategies
It reduces the total number of bugs in the system automatically
It ensures all maintenance is completed within budget
questions.rationale: The lecture notes state that understanding maintenance types 'helps developers prioritize their work and develop effective maintenance strategies' and can 'ensure software remains reliable, functional, and relevant over time by focusing on specific goals and objectives.' CAPPE provides a framework for classification and prioritization, not a guarantee of bug-free code or budget compliance.

questions.question: According to the lecture notes, which of the following is a stated purpose of software documentation such as user manuals and help files?
options: To serve as a legal contract between the developer and the client
To provide users with information they need to use the software effectively
To replace the need for software maintenance entirely
To document every line of the source code for future developers
questions.rationale: The lecture notes state that 'software documentation is essential for providing users with info they need to use the software effectively. This includes user manuals, help files, and training materials that can help users understand how to use the software, troubleshoot issues, get the most out of its features.' Documentation supports users, not the internal codebase.

questions.question: A README guideline states you should 'start with a brief overview of the project.' According to the lecture notes, what three elements should this overview include?
options: Version history, changelog, and contributors
Purpose, features, and target audience
Architecture diagram, tech stack, and deployment steps
License type, repository URL, and build status
questions.rationale: The README guidelines state: 'Provide an overview -- start with a brief overview of the project, including its purpose, features, and target audience.' This gives readers an immediate understanding of what the project does, what it offers, and who it is for, before diving into installation or usage details.

questions.question: According to the README guidelines, when is it appropriate to include screenshots or code snippets in a README?
options: Never -- READMEs should only contain plain text
When illustrating examples of how to use the software and what it can do
Only when the project has a graphical user interface
Only in a separate documentation file, not the README itself
questions.rationale: The README guidelines state: 'Provide examples -- include examples of how to use software and what it can do. Use screenshots or code snippets to illustrate your points if needed.' Screenshots and code snippets serve as visual or practical demonstrations that help users understand the software's capabilities.

questions.question: The lecture notes identify four broad reasons why software requires maintenance. Which of the following is NOT one of them?
options: Bug fixing and issue resolution
Software updates and upgrades
Rewriting the software from scratch in a new programming language
Maintenance of the codebase
questions.rationale: The four reasons are: (1) Bug fixing and issue resolution, (2) Software updates and upgrades, (3) User support and training, and (4) Maintenance of the codebase. Rewriting from scratch is not listed; maintenance is about evolving and improving existing software, not replacing it entirely.

questions.question: Software bugs can impact three key aspects of the system, according to the lecture notes. Which are they?
options: Cost, schedule, and team morale
Functionality, performance, and security
Design, testing, and deployment
Hardware, network, and storage
questions.rationale: The lecture notes state that bugs and issues 'impact its functionality, performance, and security.' Maintenance is required to identify and fix these issues to ensure the software remains reliable and meets the needs of users. These three dimensions cover what the software does, how well it does it, and how safe it is.

questions.question: A company is concerned about potential security breaches and data leaks. According to the lecture notes' specific example, which preventive maintenance actions should the team perform?
options: Add new features and redesign the user interface
Implement encryption, access controls, and firewalls; conduct security audits and penetration testing
Roll back to a previous version and implement a temporary fix
Refactor the database schema and add caching mechanisms
questions.rationale: The lecture notes give a specific preventive maintenance example: 'implementing security best practices such as encryption, access controls, and firewalls' and 'conduct regular security audits and penetration testing to identify potential vulnerabilities and address them before they can be exploited.' These are proactive measures to reduce the risk of future problems.

questions.question: Emergency maintenance can be triggered by several types of critical events. According to the lecture notes, which of the following is listed as a trigger for emergency maintenance?
options: A user requesting a new feature
A scheduled quarterly code review
A security breach requiring immediate attention
A planned database migration
questions.rationale: The lecture notes state that emergency maintenance 'may involve responding to system crashes, security breaches or other urgent issues that require immediate attention.' A security breach is explicitly listed as a trigger. Feature requests are perfective, scheduled reviews are preventive, and planned migrations are adaptive -- none require the urgency of emergency maintenance.

questions.question: According to the lecture notes, corrective maintenance for an app that crashes when clicking 'Submit' involves three specific steps. What is the correct sequence?
options: Deploy the fix, test the software, identify the root cause
Identify the root cause, make changes to fix the defect, test the updated software
Roll back the changes, implement a temporary fix, restore the system
Monitor system logs, conduct a security audit, deploy a patch
questions.rationale: The lecture notes describe corrective maintenance steps for the 'Submit' crash example as: (1) 'identifying the root cause of the problem,' (2) 'making changes to the code to fix the defect,' and (3) 'testing the updated software to ensure software is working correctly.' This is a methodical diagnose-fix-verify process.

questions.question: A team's web application works well, but users have requested a built-in dark mode. No external regulation or business expansion drives this request -- users simply want a better visual experience. Is adding dark mode perfective or adaptive maintenance?
options: Adaptive -- the team is adapting to user feedback
Perfective -- it improves usability without addressing a changed external requirement
Corrective -- the lack of dark mode is a usability defect
Preventive -- it prevents users from experiencing eye strain
questions.rationale: Perfective maintenance involves 'improving the software's speed, efficiency, or usability, OR adding new features or functionality.' Dark mode improves usability and adds a new feature. Adaptive maintenance specifically addresses changing user or business REQUIREMENTS driven by external factors ('world changes = software must adapt'). A user preference for visual comfort is an enhancement, not an external requirement change.

questions.question: A freelance developer discovers a critical bug in a client's deployed application. The developer no longer has access to the codebase because the client revoked their credentials after the contract ended. According to the responsibility framework, why can't the developer fix this issue?
options: The nature of the issue makes it a hardware problem
The developer lacks the level of authority -- only someone with access to the system can make changes
The user is responsible for all issues after deployment
The developer's role has changed to that of a user
questions.rationale: The responsibility framework identifies 'level of authority' as a key factor: 'only someone with access to the system/code can fix/make changes to the software.' Without access to the codebase, the developer cannot implement a fix regardless of their technical ability. Having admin access or codebase access determines whether fixing issues is your responsibility.

questions.question: A SaaS company experiences the following sequence: (1) their database server crashes at 2 AM, (2) the on-call team deploys a temporary fix to restore service, (3) the next morning, the team diagnoses the root cause and deploys a permanent patch, (4) they then add automated monitoring to detect similar failures early. What CAPPE types do steps 2, 3, and 4 represent?
options: Corrective, Perfective, Adaptive
Emergency, Corrective, Preventive
Emergency, Emergency, Corrective
Corrective, Preventive, Perfective
questions.rationale: Step 2 is emergency maintenance -- fixing a critical issue as quickly as possible with a temporary fix to minimize downtime. Step 3 is corrective maintenance -- properly diagnosing and fixing the root cause to restore correct functioning. Step 4 is preventive maintenance -- proactively adding monitoring to identify potential problems before they become actual failures.

questions.question: The lecture notes state that all four reasons why software requires maintenance share a common overarching purpose. What is it?
options: To reduce the development team's workload
To ensure software remains relevant and meets the needs of its users
To generate additional revenue through paid updates
To satisfy regulatory compliance requirements
questions.rationale: The lecture notes conclude the four reasons with: '*All are to ensure software remains relevant and meet needs of its users.' Whether it is bug fixing, updates, user support documentation, or codebase maintenance, the underlying goal is keeping the software useful and relevant for its users over time.

questions.question: According to the lecture notes, software codebases can become complex over time. What three maintenance activities help keep the codebase organized?
options: Adding features, deploying updates, and training users
Refactoring code, optimizing performance, and improving maintainability
Conducting security audits, monitoring logs, and performing backups
Modifying the UI, adding language support, and updating the database schema
questions.rationale: The lecture notes state: 'Maintenance of the codebase: software codebases can become complex over time... includes refactoring code, optimizing performance, and improving maintainability.' These three activities ensure the code remains organized, maintainable, and efficient so the software can continue to evolve.

questions.question: A mobile banking app has two issues. Issue A: the app sometimes displays the wrong account balance after a transfer (a calculation error). Issue B: the app is slow to load on older devices, frustrating some users. Which maintenance types address Issue A and Issue B, respectively?
options: Preventive and Adaptive
Emergency and Perfective
Corrective and Perfective
Adaptive and Corrective
questions.rationale: Issue A (wrong balance after transfer) is a software defect that must be fixed to restore correct functioning -- corrective maintenance. Issue B (slow loading on older devices) is a performance/usability issue where the app works correctly but could be improved -- perfective maintenance. Corrective restores correctness; perfective enhances quality.

questions.question: A README's overview section reads: 'TaskFlow is a lightweight project management tool designed for freelancers and small teams. It features task boards, time tracking, and client invoicing.' Does this follow the 'Provide an overview' guideline?
options: No -- it is too short and needs at least 500 words
No -- it should explain the source code architecture
Yes -- it briefly covers purpose, features, and target audience
Yes -- but only because it avoids technical jargon
questions.rationale: The guideline says: 'Start with a brief overview of the project, including its purpose, features, and target audience.' This overview names the purpose (project management tool), key features (task boards, time tracking, invoicing), and target audience (freelancers, small teams). It is concise and informative -- exactly what the guideline prescribes.

questions.question: A team has two tasks. Task X: implement automated server health checks that alert the team before CPU usage reaches critical levels. Task Y: rewrite the authentication module to use a faster hashing algorithm, reducing login time from 3 seconds to 0.5 seconds. Which CAPPE types are X and Y?
options: Corrective and Adaptive
Preventive and Perfective
Adaptive and Corrective
Perfective and Preventive
questions.rationale: Task X is preventive maintenance -- proactively monitoring for potential problems (high CPU) before they cause actual failures. Task Y is perfective maintenance -- improving the software's speed and efficiency (faster login) without fixing a defect. Preventive acts before problems manifest; perfective enhances existing quality.

questions.question: During an emergency maintenance event, the team implements a temporary fix. According to the lecture notes, what is the goal of this temporary fix?
options: To permanently resolve the underlying defect
To restore the system to working order as quickly as possible
To improve the software's performance for all users
To comply with a new regulatory requirement
questions.rationale: Emergency maintenance's goal is to 'minimize downtime and ensure that the software remains functional.' The lecture notes describe 'implementing a temporary fix to restore system to working order' as one of the typical steps. The emphasis is on speed of recovery, not on a permanent solution -- that comes later through corrective maintenance.

questions.question: A README file uses the phrase: 'The microservice employs an event-driven CQRS pattern with eventual consistency guarantees via a distributed saga orchestrator.' Why does this violate README best practices?
options: It is too short to be useful for readers
It should describe the deployment architecture instead
It uses technical jargon that non-technical users would not understand
It reveals proprietary implementation details
questions.rationale: The README guidelines state: 'Use simple and clear language that is easy to understand, even for non-technical users. Avoid using technical jargon. Not everyone is a developer.' Terms like 'CQRS pattern,' 'eventual consistency,' and 'saga orchestrator' are advanced architectural jargon that most users would not comprehend.

questions.question: Adaptive maintenance may involve modifying three aspects of the software to meet changing needs. According to the lecture notes, which three?
options: Hardware, network, and storage configurations
Features, user interface, and performance
Testing strategy, deployment pipeline, and documentation
Team structure, sprint length, and code review process
questions.rationale: The lecture notes state adaptive maintenance 'may involve modifying the software's features, user interface, or performance to meet changing needs.' These three aspects cover what the software does (features), how users interact with it (UI), and how well it operates (performance) -- all of which may need adjustment as requirements evolve.

questions.question: A logistics company's routing software calculates delivery routes correctly, but a new city ordinance bans heavy trucks from certain residential streets between 7 PM and 7 AM. The team must update the routing algorithm to account for these time-based restrictions. Is this corrective or adaptive maintenance?
options: Corrective -- the current routes now violate a law
Adaptive -- the software must be modified to meet a changed external requirement
Preventive -- the team should have anticipated this regulation
Emergency -- trucks could be fined if routes are not updated immediately
questions.rationale: The software was working correctly under the previous rules -- there is no bug. A new external requirement (the city ordinance) forces the software to adapt. This is adaptive maintenance: 'modifying the software to meet changing user or business requirements.' The world has changed, so the software must adapt. It is not corrective because the software was not defective before the regulation.

questions.question: The lecture notes state that 'software codebases can become complex over time.' Which type of maintenance addresses this growing complexity to keep the code organized and efficient?
options: Corrective -- fixing bugs introduced by complexity
Adaptive -- adapting to the complexity of new requirements
Perfective -- refactoring and improving maintainability of the codebase
Emergency -- complexity causes urgent system failures
questions.rationale: The lecture notes describe 'maintenance of the codebase' as requiring 'refactoring code, optimizing performance, and improving maintainability.' These activities align with perfective maintenance, whose goal is to 'enhance the software's capabilities and improve its quality.' Addressing codebase complexity is an improvement to internal quality, not a bug fix or external adaptation.

questions.question: A team writes a README that lists installation commands for macOS, Windows, and Linux, includes a quick-start code snippet, and provides a screenshot of the application's main dashboard. Which README guidelines does this satisfy?
options: Only 'Keep it concise' and 'Use clear language'
'Explain installation and usage' and 'Provide examples'
Only 'Provide contact information' and 'Update regularly'
'Provide an overview' and 'Update regularly'
questions.rationale: Cross-platform installation commands with a quick-start snippet satisfy 'Explain installation and usage -- explain with clear instructions on how to install and use software.' A code snippet and a screenshot satisfy 'Provide examples -- include examples of how to use software and what it can do. Use screenshots or code snippets to illustrate your points if needed.'

questions.question: A company's HR system handles three concurrent issues: (1) a payroll calculation is producing incorrect totals, (2) a new tax law requires updated withholding rates by next quarter, (3) the annual security audit is due. Which CAPPE combination correctly classifies all three?
options: Perfective, Adaptive, Emergency
Corrective, Adaptive, Preventive
Corrective, Corrective, Preventive
Corrective, Perfective, Adaptive
questions.rationale: Issue 1 (incorrect payroll totals) is a defect requiring corrective maintenance. Issue 2 (new tax law requiring updated rates) is a changing external requirement requiring adaptive maintenance. Issue 3 (scheduled security audit) is a proactive activity to identify potential problems requiring preventive maintenance.

questions.question: The lecture notes emphasize that 'software evolves over time.' Which of the following best explains why this evolution creates an ongoing need for maintenance?
options: Software always degrades in quality regardless of maintenance
Evolution requires updates, upgrades, and adaptations to keep the software up-to-date and meeting user needs
Evolving software automatically generates new documentation
Software evolution only occurs when bugs are discovered
questions.rationale: The lecture notes state: 'software evolves over time, updates and upgrades are often required to add new features, improve performance, or address security vulnerabilities. Maintenance is required to implement these updates and upgrades to ensure that the software remains up-to-date and can continue to meet needs of its users.' Evolution encompasses far more than just bug fixing.

questions.question: A team evaluates five tasks and must classify each under CAPPE. Which row has ALL five classifications correct? Task 1: Fix a broken login button. Task 2: Add support for a new national ID format required by law. Task 3: Optimise image loading speed. Task 4: Schedule nightly database backups. Task 5: Restore a crashed production server within minutes.
options: Corrective, Adaptive, Perfective, Preventive, Emergency
Corrective, Corrective, Adaptive, Perfective, Emergency
Emergency, Adaptive, Perfective, Preventive, Corrective
Corrective, Adaptive, Preventive, Perfective, Emergency
questions.rationale: Task 1 (broken login button) = Corrective -- fixing a defect. Task 2 (new legal ID format) = Adaptive -- modifying for a changed external requirement. Task 3 (optimise image speed) = Perfective -- improving performance. Task 4 (nightly backups) = Preventive -- proactively reducing risk of data loss. Task 5 (restore crashed server ASAP) = Emergency -- minimising downtime on a critical failure. The full CAPPE spectrum is represented.

questions.question: A startup has finished building and testing its web application. The CTO asks: 'What comes next in the software lifecycle?' According to the deployment lifecycle, what is the correct sequence of remaining stages?
options: Maintain --> Deploy
Deploy --> Maintain
Design --> Deploy
Test --> Design --> Deploy
questions.rationale: The software lifecycle is: Design --> Develop --> Test --> Deploy --> Maintain. After building (Develop) and testing (Test), the next stage is Deploy, followed by Maintain. Deployment makes the system available for use; maintenance keeps it running over time.

questions.question: A team is preparing their application for deployment. They are determining the system resources needed for acceptable performance and documenting their deployment activities. Which deployment activity is this?
options: Installation and Activation
Release
Update
Deactivation
questions.rationale: Release is preparing the software to be deployed. It 'includes all operations to prepare a system for assembly and transfer to the computer system(s) on which it will be run in production.' It sometimes involves 'determining system resources needed for acceptable performance and planning or documenting deployment activities.'

questions.question: A system administrator runs an installation script that configures the database, sets up environment variables, and creates a desktop shortcut. They then start the application for the very first time. Which two deployment activities just occurred?
options: Release and Update
Deactivation and Uninstallation
Installation and Activation
Update and Built-in Update
questions.rationale: Installation involves 'establishing some form of a command, shortcut, script or service for executing the software' and may involve 'configuration of the system.' Activation is the 'activity of starting up the executable component of software for the first time.' The script performed installation; starting the app for the first time is activation.

questions.question: A DevOps team needs to deploy a critical security patch to their production server. Before they can install the update, they must first shut down the currently running application. Which deployment activity must occur before the update, and why?
options: Uninstallation -- the old version must be completely removed first
Deactivation -- the system may need to be deactivated before an update can be performed
Release -- the patch must be prepared for production first
Activation -- the new version must be started before the old one is stopped
questions.rationale: Deactivation is the 'inverse of activation and refers to shutting down any already executing components of a system.' The lecture notes state: 'Deactivation is often required to perform other deployment activities. E.g. software system may need to be deactivated before update can be performed.'

questions.question: A company decides to retire a legacy payroll system that has not been used in over two years. They plan to shut it down permanently and remove it. What is this practice called according to the lecture notes?
options: Emergency maintenance
Blue-green deployment
Application retirement / application decommissioning
Canary release rollback
questions.rationale: The lecture notes state: 'The practice of removing infrequently used/obsolete systems from service is often referred to as application retirement/application decommissioning.' This is specifically the role of an IT auditor. It involves deactivation (shutting down) and often uninstallation of the retired system.

questions.question: When uninstalling a software system, the lecture notes highlight a concern beyond simply removing files. The team must also reconfigure other systems that depended on the removed software. What is the other main modern concern for performing deactivation and uninstallation?
options: Reducing cloud hosting costs
Improving application performance
Security concerns
Freeing up developer time for new projects
questions.rationale: Uninstallation 'may also involve some reconfiguration of other software system to remove the uninstalled system dependencies.' The lecture notes add: 'The other main concern nowadays why we need to perform deactivation and uninstallation is due to security concerns.' Unused or obsolete systems can become security vulnerabilities.

questions.question: A Linux server administrator runs 'apt upgrade' to replace the current version of a package with a newer release. The package manager automatically removes the old version and installs the new one. According to the lecture notes, an update commonly consists of which two deployment activities?
options: Release followed by Activation
Deactivation followed by Installation
Uninstallation followed by Release
Installation followed by Deactivation
questions.rationale: The lecture notes state: 'Update process replaces an earlier version of all or part of a software system with a newer release. Commonly consists of deactivation followed by installation.' With package managers like apt, 'the old version is usually automatically removed and replaced by the new version.'

questions.question: Google Chrome periodically downloads and applies patches without the user initiating anything. Windows Update can also install security fixes automatically. Which deployment activity do these represent?
options: Manual update via package manager
Built-in update
Blue-green deployment
Canary release
questions.rationale: Built-in updates are 'mechanisms for installing updates built into some software systems.' The 'automation of these update processes ranges from fully automatic to user-initiated and controlled.' Chrome auto-update and Windows Update are the two examples given in the lecture notes.

questions.question: A development team uses Git to manage their codebase. Developers create separate branches for features, bug fixes, and releases. What does this practice ensure according to the lecture notes?
options: That all code is written in a single branch for simplicity
That stable versions can be released and deployed safely
That testing is no longer necessary before deployment
That only one developer can work on the code at a time
questions.rationale: Version tracking 'uses version control systems (e.g., Git) to manage software changes. Developers create branches for features, fixes, and releases. Ensures stable versions can be released and deployed safely.' Branching allows parallel development while keeping the release branch stable.

questions.question: A developer is writing and testing code on their own laptop. According to the deployment environments, which environment are they working in?
options: Development/Trunk
Integration
Local
Staging
questions.rationale: The Local environment is the 'developer's workstation' used for 'coding and testing.' It is the most personal environment where developers write and run code before pushing it to any shared environment.

questions.question: After pushing their code, developers merge into a shared server that acts as a sandbox where the team tests code together and unit testing may be performed. Which environment is this?
options: Local
Staging
Development/Trunk
Production
questions.rationale: The Development/Trunk environment is a 'shared environment where developers test code together.' It is described as a 'development server that acts as a sandbox where unit testing may be performed by developer (more on software).' It is the first shared environment after individual local workstations.

questions.question: A team needs to verify that their microservices can communicate correctly with each other and with external APIs. They deploy to an environment specifically designed to check if components can interact. Which environment is this?
options: Local
Development/Trunk
Integration
Production
questions.rationale: The Integration environment is used to 'check if components can interact and work with one another.' It is described as a 'CI build target, or for testing of side effects (more on hardware).' It focuses on inter-component compatibility rather than individual unit correctness.

questions.question: The QA team deploys a build to an environment where they verify the system works, check for bugs, confirm requirements, and ensure new code does not impact existing functionality. Which environment is this?
options: Integration
Staging
Testing / QC / Internal Acceptance
Production
questions.rationale: The Testing/Test/QC/Internal Acceptance environment is where 'interface testing is done,' to 'verify system works, check for bugs, confirm requirements.' The quality control team 'ensures new code will not impact existing functionality' and 'test major functionalities of the system after deployment.'

questions.question: Before releasing to real users, a team deploys their application to an environment that mirrors production exactly. They treat it as a 'rehearsal before the live show.' Which environment is this?
options: Development/Trunk
Testing/QC
Staging
Integration
questions.rationale: The Staging environment is for 'final testing before release. Think of it as rehearsal before live show.' It is described as a 'mirror of production environment,' meaning it replicates the production setup as closely as possible to catch environment-specific issues before going live.

questions.question: An e-commerce company follows the DTAP deployment pipeline. What does DTAP stand for, and what is the correct order?
options: Design, Testing, Analysis, Production
Development, Testing, Acceptance, Production
Deployment, Testing, Activation, Production
Development, Tracking, Acceptance, Publishing
questions.rationale: DTAP stands for 'Deployment, testing, acceptance and production' and is 'a phased approach to software testing and deployment (common deployment pipeline).' The correct flow is: Development --> Testing --> Acceptance --> Production.

questions.question: A company's deployment pipeline is: DEV --> TEST --> ACCEPTANCE --> PROD --> BACKUP. The 'BACKUP' environment at the end is used for what purpose?
options: Training new users on the production system
Running automated performance tests
Disaster recovery
A/B testing new features
questions.rationale: The Backup environment is a 'backup site which is used for disaster recovery.' Some organizations extend the standard DTAP pipeline with additional environments. The Education environment is for training users; Backup is specifically for disaster recovery.

questions.question: Traditionally, system administrators manually configured servers, leading to inconsistent environments and configuration errors. A tool called Ansible addresses this by enabling what concept?
options: Manual deployment checklists
Infrastructure as Code (IaC)
Blue-green deployment
Canary release automation
questions.rationale: Traditional manual setup 'results in inconsistent environments and config errors.' Ansible addresses these as 'a suite of software tools that enables infrastructure as code (IaC).' It is 'open source and the suite includes software provisioning, configuration management, and application deployment functionality.'

questions.question: A company runs two identical production environments. Version 1.0 (blue) serves all current traffic. They deploy Version 2.0 to the second environment (green), then switch ALL traffic from blue to green at once. If issues arise, they switch back to blue instantly. Which deployment strategy is this?
options: Canary release
A/B testing
Blue-green deployment
Feature flag rollout
questions.rationale: Blue-green deployment is 'an application release model that gradually transfers user traffic from a previous version (blue) to a nearly identical new release (green). Both are running in production.' Traffic switches 'all at once,' and blue 'can standby in case of rollback.' The hallmark is two full environments with an instant switch.

questions.question: After a blue-green deployment, all traffic is now on the green (new) environment. According to the lecture notes, what are the two possible fates of the blue (old) environment?
options: It is permanently deleted, or archived to cold storage
It stands by for rollback, or is pulled from production to become the template for the next update
It becomes the staging environment, or is converted to a backup
It is immediately updated to match green, or sold to another company
questions.rationale: The lecture notes state that once traffic is fully on green, 'blue can standby in case of rollback or pulled from production to be updated to be template for next update.' This means blue either stays ready as a safety net or gets recycled for the next deployment cycle.

questions.question: A social media company deploys a new recommendation algorithm to only 5% of its users first. They monitor performance metrics and user feedback. If everything looks good, they gradually increase to 25%, then 50%, then 100%. Which deployment strategy is this?
options: Blue-green deployment
Canary release
A/B testing
DTAP pipeline
questions.rationale: Canary releases are a 'deployment strategy whereby changes are initially released to a small subset of users.' They allow 'gradual rollout and monitoring' with a 'gradual % rollout.' Failures have 'limited impact because the new version is released to a small subset of users first,' unlike blue-green which 'switches all users to the new version at once.'

questions.question: According to the comparison table in the lecture notes, which deployment strategy has LOWER risk and does NOT require two full production environments?
options: Blue-green deployment -- because rollback is instant
Canary release -- it has very low risk and can run within the same infrastructure
A/B testing -- it only deploys to a control group
DTAP -- it separates development from production
questions.rationale: The comparison table shows: Blue-green has medium risk and requires two full environments. Canary has very low risk and 'can run within same infrastructure.' Canary is also smoother -- 'failures have limited impact' because only a small subset of users see the new version initially.

questions.question: A canary deployment offers five key benefits. One benefit states: 'It is impossible to test capacity of a large production environment, but with canary deployments, capacity tests are built-in.' What does this mean in practice?
options: Canary deployments automatically run load testing scripts
Performance issues emerge naturally as users are gradually migrated to the new version
The canary environment has double the server capacity of production
Capacity is pre-calculated using PERT estimation formulas
questions.rationale: The lecture notes explain: 'With Canary deployments, capacity tests are built-in. Any performance issues we have in our system will begin to crop up as we slowly migrate users to the canary.' The gradual migration effectively stress-tests the new version under real production load incrementally.

questions.question: A product team wants to test whether a redesigned checkout page converts more sales than the current design. They deploy both versions simultaneously to different user groups and measure conversion rates. Which technique is this?
options: Canary release
Blue-green deployment
A/B testing
Feature flag
questions.rationale: A/B testing is 'deploying 2 versions of your software to different groups of users to test which version performs better.' It is 'useful when you want to test new feature/design changes before rolling them out to all users.' The goal is comparison (which is better?), not gradual rollout.

questions.question: A team releases a new 'dark mode' feature but only enables it for premium users initially. If bugs appear, they can disable it instantly without redeploying the application. Which technique are they using?
options: Blue-green deployment
Canary release
A/B testing
Feature flags
questions.rationale: Feature flags 'allow us to enable/disable certain features of our software for specific users or groups of users without redeploying.' They are 'useful when you want to test new features or release features gradually.' Key benefits include: gradual rollout, testing features, and quick disable if bugs appear.

questions.question: A project has the following PERT estimates for a task: Optimistic time (O) = 4 days, Most Likely time (M) = 7 days, Pessimistic time (P) = 16 days. Using the PERT formula (O + 4M + P) / 6, what is the expected duration?
options: 7 days
8 days
9 days
10 days
questions.rationale: The PERT formula is: (O + 4M + P) / 6 = (4 + 4(7) + 16) / 6 = (4 + 28 + 16) / 6 = 48 / 6 = 8 days. The formula weights the most likely time (M) four times more heavily than the optimistic (O) and pessimistic (P) estimates.

questions.question: A project has two parallel paths. Path 1 (A --> B --> D --> E) takes 22 days. Path 2 (A --> C --> D --> E) takes 18 days. According to PERT, which is the critical path and what is the minimum project duration?
options: Path 2 (18 days) -- shorter paths finish first
Path 1 (22 days) -- the critical path is the longest path
Both paths (40 days) -- durations are added together
Neither -- parallel paths cancel each other out
questions.rationale: The critical path is the 'longest sequence of dependent activities that determines the minimum project duration.' Path 1 at 22 days is longer than Path 2 at 18 days, so Path 1 is the critical path. The project cannot finish faster than 22 days because Path 1's tasks must all complete. Path 2 has 'slack time' while waiting for Path 1.

questions.question: A task has a PERT standard deviation of 0.5 days (O = 5, P = 8). Another task has a standard deviation of 4 days (O = 2, P = 26). What does this tell a project manager about the risk associated with each task?
options: Both tasks have the same level of risk since they use the same PERT formula
The first task is high risk (large deviation); the second is low risk
The first task is low risk (high confidence); the second is high risk (high uncertainty)
Standard deviation has no relationship to project risk
questions.rationale: Low standard deviation (low risk) means 'your Best Case (O) and Worst Case (P) are very close -- you are highly confident the project will finish on time.' High standard deviation (high risk) means 'there is a massive gap between Best Case and Worst Case -- the task is complex or experimental; you are basically guessing.' Task 1 (SD=0.5) is low risk; Task 2 (SD=4) is high risk.

questions.question: In PERT, what is the relationship between an 'Activity,' an 'Event (Node),' and a 'Dependency'?
options: An Activity is a milestone; an Event is a task; a Dependency is a time estimate
An Activity is a task to be completed; an Event is a milestone marking start or end; a Dependency means one task must finish before another starts
All three terms mean the same thing in different contexts
An Activity links two Events; a Dependency is the shortest path between them
questions.rationale: The PERT terms are: Activity = 'a task that must be completed.' Event (Node) = 'a milestone marking the start or end of activity (need to know sequence).' Dependency = 'relationship where one task must finish before another starts.' These form the building blocks of a PERT network diagram.

questions.question: Project planning becomes difficult under three conditions, according to the lecture notes. PERT helps address these challenges. Which set of conditions is correct?
options: Budget overruns, team conflicts, and scope creep
Many tasks depend on each other, task duration is uncertain, and delays in one task affect others
Hardware failures, network outages, and security breaches
Unclear requirements, frequent design changes, and poor documentation
questions.rationale: The lecture notes state that 'project planning becomes difficult when: many tasks depend on each other, task duration is uncertain, delays in one task affect others.' PERT addresses these by handling uncertain durations, visualising workflow, identifying the critical path, and predicting minimum completion time.

questions.question: A deployment pipeline includes an 'Education' environment alongside the standard DTAP stages. According to the lecture notes, what is the purpose of this Education environment?
options: For developers to learn new programming languages
For training users of the production environment
For testing experimental features that may never ship
For onboarding new team members to the codebase
questions.rationale: The Education environment is 'for training users' and is described as 'an extension of the DTAP-street -- a training environment for users of the production environment.' It is one of the additional environments organizations may add alongside Backup (for disaster recovery).

questions.question: A team debates which deployment strategy to use for a high-stakes financial platform update. Strategy X requires two full production environments and switches all traffic simultaneously -- rollback is an instant switch back. Strategy Y releases to 5% of users first and can roll back by reducing traffic. Which strategies are X and Y?
options: X is Canary, Y is Blue-Green
X is Blue-Green, Y is Canary
X is A/B testing, Y is Blue-Green
X is Feature flags, Y is Canary
questions.rationale: Blue-Green deployment requires 'two full environments,' switches traffic 'all at once,' and rollback is an 'instant switch' back to the old environment. Canary releases to 'a small subset of users' first (gradual % rollout) and rollback means 'reduce traffic to new version.' X matches Blue-Green; Y matches Canary.

questions.question: A company's new microservice occasionally experiences 'cold start' slowness -- the service takes 30 seconds to warm up when first launched under load. Which deployment strategy specifically addresses this problem as a named benefit?
options: Blue-green deployment -- two warm environments are always running
Canary releases -- they 'slowly build up momentum to prevent cold-start slowness'
A/B testing -- warm-up is split across two user groups
Feature flags -- the feature can be enabled gradually
questions.rationale: One of the five named benefits of canary deployments is: 'No cold starts: new systems can take a while to start up. Canary deployments slowly build up momentum to prevent cold-start slowness.' By gradually shifting traffic, the new system warms up incrementally rather than receiving a sudden full production load.

questions.question: A product team deploys a new UI to 50% of users and the old UI to the other 50% to measure which produces more sign-ups. After two weeks they will roll out the winner to everyone. This is different from a canary release because the goal is comparison, not gradual rollout. Which technique is this?
options: Canary release
Blue-green deployment
Feature flags
A/B testing
questions.rationale: A/B testing is 'deploying 2 versions of your software to different groups of users to test which version performs better.' It is 'useful when you want to test new feature/design changes before rolling them out to all users.' The purpose is measuring comparative performance, not gradually migrating all users to a new version.

questions.question: A company deploys a feature flag for a new payments module. Two weeks later a critical bug is discovered in it. The engineering team disables the flag immediately without any redeployment. Which feature flag benefit does this demonstrate?
options: Gradual rollout
Testing features
Quick disable if bugs appear
No downtime upgrades
questions.rationale: Feature flags provide three key benefits: gradual rollout, testing features, and 'quick disable if bugs appear.' Being able to instantly disable a buggy feature without redeploying is the 'quick disable' benefit -- the code is still deployed but the feature is toggled off remotely.

questions.question: According to the lecture notes, deployment activities can occur on which sides of the process?
options: Only on the producer (development team) side
Only on the consumer (end-user) side
On the producer side, consumer side, or both
Only on the infrastructure team side
questions.rationale: The lecture notes state that the general deployment process 'consists of several interrelated activities with possible transitions between them. These activities occur on producer side/consumer side or both.' For example, installation may be performed by the consumer (user installing software), while release is performed by the producer (the development team).

questions.question: A deployment template is described in the lecture notes. Which of the following best describes what a deployment template is?
options: A filled-in, ready-to-execute deployment plan for a specific release
An unbound deployment plan that defines the steps of execution
A post-deployment report documenting what was deployed
A version control branch strategy document
questions.rationale: A deployment template is 'an unbound deployment plan which defines the steps of execution.' It is a 'structured document' that organisations create to standardise their deployment process. It is 'unbound' because it is a template -- not yet tied to a specific release -- that gets filled in to create a concrete deployment plan.

questions.question: A team releases a hotfix using package managers on a Linux system. They run 'apt install myapp=2.1'. The system automatically removes version 2.0 and replaces it with 2.1. According to the lecture notes, what type of update was this, and what two activities did it consist of?
options: A built-in update; Release then Activation
A package-manager update; Deactivation of the old version then Installation of the new version
A manual update; Uninstallation of the old version then Installation of the new version
A blue-green update; Switching traffic from old to new environment
questions.rationale: The lecture notes state: 'Update process replaces an earlier version... commonly consists of deactivation followed by installation.' When 'software is updated using package managers (e.g., in Linux system), the old version is usually automatically removed and replaced by the new version.' The automatic removal is effectively deactivation; the new version install is installation.

questions.question: The lecture notes state that the precise processes within each deployment activity 'can be hardly defined.' What is the implication of this for how deployment should be approached?
options: Deployment should be skipped for small projects to save time
Deployment must be customised according to specific system requirements and characteristics
All teams should use the same standard deployment procedure regardless of project
Deployment activities should be fully automated with no human involvement
questions.rationale: The lecture notes state: 'As every software system is unique, the precise processes or procedures within each activity can be hardly defined. Thus, deployment should be interpreted as a general process that has to be customized according to specific requirements/characteristics.' There is no universal deployment procedure.

questions.question: A project manager must schedule six activities: Requirements Analysis (A), System Design (B), UI Design (C), Coding (D), Testing (E), and Deployment (F). C and B can run in parallel after A. D can only start after both B and C finish. E follows D; F follows E. Which sequence represents the critical path if B takes 7 days and C takes 3 days?
options: A --> C --> D --> E --> F (shorter path is critical)
A --> B --> D --> E --> F (longer of the parallel branches is critical)
Both paths have equal duration and share the critical path
A --> D --> E --> F (B and C can be skipped)
questions.rationale: The critical path is the 'longest sequence of dependent activities.' Since D cannot start until BOTH B and C finish, the bottleneck is whichever takes longer. B (7 days) > C (3 days), so Path A-->B-->D-->E-->F is the critical path. Path A-->C-->D-->E-->F has 'slack time' (4 days) while waiting for B to complete.

questions.question: A task has PERT estimates of O=2, M=5, P=8. A second task has O=1, M=5, P=15. Both have the same most-likely time (M=5). Why would a project manager treat these two tasks differently in terms of scheduling risk?
options: They would not -- same M value means identical risk
Task 1 has a narrower O-P range (lower standard deviation, lower risk); Task 2 has a wider O-P range (higher standard deviation, higher uncertainty)
Task 2 is lower risk because its optimistic estimate is shorter
Task 1 is higher risk because it has a smaller pessimistic estimate
