It 'provides an organized view of configuration data and a means of examining that data from any desired perspective.'

questions.question: According to the lecture notes, CIs are the focal point of the CMDB. Without a clear definition of what qualifies as a CI, the team will face what specific challenge?
options: They will deploy software to the wrong environment
They will struggle with deciding whether to put certain kinds of data into the CMDB
They will lose access to the source code repository
They will be unable to perform any software testing
questions.rationale: The lecture notes state: 'Configuration items (CIs) are the focal point of CMDB. Without a clear definition of what qualifies as a CI, we will struggle with deciding whether to put certain kinds of data into the CMDB.' Clear CI definitions are essential for maintaining a useful and consistent CMDB.

questions.question: A customer buys a new laptop. The manufacturer guarantees to repair or replace it if it develops a hardware defect within 2 years. According to the lecture notes, what is the goal of this guarantee?
options: To increase the manufacturer's revenue through extended service plans
To reassure the customer that their investment will last and support is available if it doesn't work
To require the customer to use only manufacturer-approved parts
To transfer all maintenance costs to the customer
questions.rationale: The goal of a warranty is 'customer assurance: to reassure the customer that their investment will last and support is available if it doesn't work.' A warranty is a guarantee to fix the asset 'if some great damage happens OR if asset doesn't run up to its full potential.'

questions.question: The Magnuson-Moss Warranty Act is a U.S. federal law governing consumer product warranties. According to the lecture notes, which statement about this law is correct?
options: It requires every product sold in the U.S. to have a warranty
It does NOT require any product to have a warranty (products may be sold 'as-is'), but it prohibits misleading consumers
It only applies to software products, not physical goods
It allows manufacturers to force customers to use specific brands of parts
questions.rationale: The Magnuson-Moss Warranty Act requires manufacturers to 'provide clear, detailed information about warranty coverage.' Crucially, 'the law does not require any product to have a warranty (may be sold as-is).' It 'prohibits companies from misleading consumers and generally bans tie-in sales provisions that force customers to use specific brands of parts or services.'

questions.question: A car owner pays for regular oil changes and tyre rotations every 6 months. Separately, the car's engine is covered by the manufacturer's 5-year guarantee against defects. According to the lecture notes, which is the warranty and which is maintenance?
options: Oil changes are warranty; the engine guarantee is maintenance
Both are forms of warranty provided by the manufacturer
The engine guarantee is warranty (fix if damaged or underperforming); the oil changes are maintenance (routine service at the owner's cost)
Both are forms of maintenance paid for by the owner
questions.rationale: Warranty is a 'guarantee to fix your asset if some great damage happens OR if asset doesn't run up to its full potential.' Maintenance is 'a routine service done to your asset that typically comes out of own pocket.' The manufacturer's engine guarantee is warranty; the owner-paid oil changes are maintenance.

questions.question: An SCM team identifies functional and physical attributes of their software at the start of a project and then monitors how those attributes change over the next 18 months. What SCM capability does this demonstrate?
options: SCM only tracks changes at the moment of deployment
SCM identifies attributes at various points in time and tracks them over time
SCM replaces the need for version control systems like Git
SCM only tracks physical hardware, not software attributes
questions.rationale: The lecture notes state: 'SCM tracks system attributes over time: SCM identifies functional and physical attributes of software at various points in time.' This longitudinal tracking is essential for maintaining integrity and traceability throughout the SDLC.

questions.question: Within the 4 core SCM activities, a team reviews all components before a release to verify that the final build is correct and complete. Which activity is this?
options: Configuration identification
Configuration control
Configuration status accounting
Configuration audits
questions.rationale: Configuration audits 'check if everything is correct.' Reviewing components before release to verify completeness and correctness is an audit activity. This differs from identification (defining what to track), control (managing/approving changes), and status accounting (recording component status).

questions.question: A developer submits a code change. Before it can be merged, it must go through an approval process where a lead reviews and approves or rejects the change. Which core SCM activity governs this approval workflow?
options: Configuration identification
Configuration control
Configuration status accounting
Configuration audits
questions.rationale: Configuration control 'manages and approves changes.' The approval workflow where changes are reviewed and either approved or rejected before being incorporated is the essence of configuration control -- ensuring that all changes are managed systematically.

questions.question: At the start of a project, the team lists all components that need to be tracked: the application server, the database, the API gateway, and three microservices. Which core SCM activity is being performed?
options: Configuration identification
Configuration control
Configuration status accounting
Configuration audits
questions.rationale: Configuration identification 'defines and identifies what needs to be tracked.' Listing all components that require tracking at the project start is the identification activity -- establishing the baseline of items that will be managed throughout the project lifecycle.

questions.question: A CI candidate meets the following criteria: it has monetary value, it would be impacted by change requests, changes to it must be tracked, it is critical for compliance, and the team (not a third party) manages it. According to the CI eligibility matrix, should it be classified as a CI?
options: No -- meeting some criteria is not sufficient; all criteria must score 'high'
Yes -- it satisfies multiple eligibility criteria including cost/value, change considerations, traceability, governance, and direct management
No -- only physical hardware can be classified as a CI
It depends solely on whether it has configurable attributes
questions.rationale: The CI eligibility matrix evaluates candidates against multiple criteria: Cost/Value, Change considerations, Traceability, Governance & compliance, Management of service commitments, Maintainability, Delivery cost & quality, and Others (including whether 'we, and not a third party manage the CI candidate'). Satisfying multiple criteria strongly supports CI classification.

questions.question: The Magnuson-Moss Warranty Act generally bans 'tie-in sales' provisions. In practice, what does this mean for a consumer?
options: Consumers must purchase extended warranties for all products
Manufacturers cannot force customers to use specific brands of parts or services to maintain warranty coverage
Consumers are tied to the original retailer for all repairs
Products sold without warranties receive automatic government-backed coverage
questions.rationale: The Act 'prohibits companies from misleading consumers and generally bans tie-in sales provisions that force customers to use specific brands of parts or services to maintain warranty coverage.' This means a consumer can use third-party parts or repair services without voiding the warranty.

questions.question: CM in ITILv3 is defined as a process responsible for maintaining information about configuration items required to deliver an IT service. According to the lecture notes, this information is managed throughout what scope?
options: Only during the initial deployment phase
Throughout the lifecycle of the CI
Only when the CI is being actively modified
Only during annual compliance audits
questions.rationale: ITILv3 defines CM as 'process responsible for maintaining information about configuration items required to deliver an IT service, including their relationships.' The lecture notes specify: 'This information is managed throughout the lifecycle of the CI.' CM is an ongoing process, not a one-time activity.

questions.question: A team maintains a dashboard showing each tracked component's current version, last change date, change author, and approval status. Which core SCM activity does this dashboard support?
options: Configuration identification
Configuration control
Configuration status accounting
Configuration audits
questions.rationale: Configuration status accounting 'tracks and records the status of components.' A dashboard showing version, change dates, authors, and approval status is a direct implementation of status accounting -- providing visibility into the current state of all tracked configuration items.

questions.question: A hospital IT department tracks its MRI machine (serial: MRI-001, location: Ward 3, last serviced: Jan 2025) in their CMDB. They also store the manufacturer's product brochure about MRI machines in a separate library. According to the CI definition, which is the CI and which is not?
options: The brochure is a CI; the specific MRI machine is not
The specific installed MRI machine (MRI-001) is a CI; the generic product brochure is not
Both are CIs because both contain useful information
Neither is a CI because medical equipment is not an IT asset
questions.rationale: A CI is 'an instance of an entity that is part of our environment and has configurable attributes specific to that instance.' MRI-001 is a physical instance in their environment with specific configurable attributes (serial number, location, service date). The generic product brochure is information ABOUT the type of machine -- not a direct part of their environment. Like a software package vs an installed instance.

questions.question: A company uses ITIL for IT service management. Their CMDB stores data about all their CIs. According to the lecture notes, what makes the CMDB useful beyond simply listing components?
options: It automatically fixes configuration errors when detected
It provides an organised view of configuration data and a means of examining it from any desired perspective
It generates automated deployment scripts for all listed components
It replaces the need for version control systems
questions.rationale: The CMDB 'provides an organized view of configuration data and a means of examining that data from any desired perspective.' This means teams can query and analyse configuration data in multiple ways -- for example, 'which services depend on this server?' or 'what changed in the past 30 days?' The relationships between components make this multi-perspective analysis possible.

questions.question: An IT team is onboarding a new cloud-based HR system (SaaS). They do not own the servers; the vendor hosts everything. According to the ITIL service definition, is this arrangement a 'service'? What is the key reason?
options: No -- a service must be hosted on company-owned infrastructure
Yes -- it delivers value (HR functionality) to customers without them owning the specific costs and risks (servers, infrastructure)
No -- software is a product, not a service
Yes -- any software with a subscription model qualifies as a service
questions.rationale: A service is 'a means of delivering value to customers by facilitating outcomes customers want to achieve, but without the ownership of specific costs and risks.' The SaaS HR system delivers HR management value without the company owning servers, infrastructure costs, or uptime risks. This exactly matches the AWS example from the notes.

questions.question: An SCM team member says: 'I need to check who approved the change to the database schema on March 5th, what exactly was changed, and why.' Which SCM property makes this level of historical inquiry possible?
options: Integrity -- the system remains consistent
Traceability -- every change is tracked (who, what, when)
Configuration identification -- components are defined upfront
Configuration audits -- periodic correctness checks
questions.rationale: SCM ensures 'Traceability: can track every change (who what when).' The ability to answer 'who approved it, what changed, and when' is the definition of traceability. Integrity ensures the system stays correct; identification defines what to track; audits check correctness -- none specifically provide change history lookup.

questions.question: A software team is nearing a release. The SCM lead runs a pre-release check to confirm that all 52 planned features are included in the build and no unplanned changes crept in. Which of the two SCM process purposes does this activity represent?
options: Trace changes -- tracking all notifications made to the system
Verify final software -- ensuring the release includes all planned features
Configuration control -- managing and approving changes
Configuration identification -- defining what needs to be tracked
questions.rationale: The two SCM process purposes are: (1) Trace changes and (2) Verify final software -- 'ensure the release includes all planned features.' Confirming all 52 features are in the build directly fulfils the verify purpose: 'SCM makes sure nothing is missing and nothing is messed up in the final product.'

questions.question: A company's CMDB lists their CRM application server, the database instance it connects to, and the network firewall protecting both. It also records that the CRM depends on the database, and the database is protected by the firewall. What type of information do the dependency records represent?
options: Configuration identification records
The relationships between components -- a key part of what CMDB stores
Configuration audit results
Service warranty documentation
questions.rationale: The CMDB 'contains all relevant information about the hardware and software components used in an organization's IT services AND the relationships between those components.' The CRM-to-database dependency and database-to-firewall relationship are exactly the relationship data that makes a CMDB valuable for impact analysis.

questions.question: CM was originally developed for military engineering to manage complex weapon and information systems. According to the lecture notes, what three types of attributes does CM establish consistency for?
options: Cost, schedule, and quality attributes
Performance, functional, and physical attributes
Security, reliability, and availability attributes
Design, development, and deployment attributes
questions.rationale: Configuration management is the 'process for establishing consistency of a product: performance, functional, and physical attributes with its requirements, design, and operational information throughout its life.' These three attribute types -- performance (how it behaves), functional (what it does), and physical (what it is made of) -- cover the complete product specification.

questions.question: A customer buys software and the vendor says: 'This product is sold AS-IS. We provide no warranty.' According to the Magnuson-Moss Warranty Act, is this legal?
options: No -- all software must carry a minimum warranty by law
Yes -- the law does not require any product to have a warranty; products may be sold as-is
No -- selling software without a warranty violates consumer protection law
Yes -- but only for software costing less than $100
questions.rationale: The Magnuson-Moss Warranty Act requires manufacturers to 'provide clear, detailed information about warranty coverage' when a warranty exists, but 'the law does not require any product to have a warranty (may be sold as-is).' Selling a product without any warranty is legally permissible under this Act.

questions.question: A company purchases an enterprise software licence and discovers the vendor requires them to use only the vendor's own support services for maintenance -- otherwise the warranty is voided. According to the Magnuson-Moss Warranty Act, is this restriction legal?
options: Yes -- vendors can set any warranty conditions they choose
No -- it generally bans tie-in sales provisions that force customers to use specific brands of parts or services to maintain warranty coverage
Yes -- software licences are exempt from the Magnuson-Moss Act
No -- all software warranties must be unlimited in duration
questions.rationale: The Magnuson-Moss Warranty Act 'generally bans tie-in sales provisions that force customers to use specific brands of parts or services to maintain warranty coverage.' The vendor requiring exclusive use of their own support services to maintain the warranty is exactly the type of tie-in sales provision the Act prohibits.

questions.question: A company subscribes to Google Cloud. Every month they pay Google for computing resources. They do NOT own the servers or manage infrastructure. When a server fails, Google handles it. According to the lecture notes, what does this arrangement demonstrate about 'software as a service'?
options: Modern software increasingly requires customers to own their infrastructure
SaaS means the developer must maintain the service (servers and uptime) forever, not just finish the code once
Cloud services replace the need for any configuration management
This is an example of adaptive maintenance rather than a service
questions.rationale: The lecture notes state: 'Modern software is increasingly service-based (SaaS). This changes how we develop it because we have to maintain the service (the servers and uptime) forever, not just finish the code once.' Google Cloud exemplifies this -- Google must maintain continuous uptime and service quality as an ongoing commitment.

questions.question: A car owner pays a mechanic $200 for a routine service (oil change, filter replacement, tyre rotation) that they scheduled themselves. Later that year, the engine fails due to a manufacturing defect and the manufacturer fixes it for free under guarantee. Which transaction is maintenance and which is warranty?
options: Both are warranty -- the manufacturer covers all repairs
The routine service ($200 out of pocket) is maintenance; the free engine fix under guarantee is warranty
The routine service is warranty; the engine fix is a form of corrective maintenance
Both are maintenance -- routine upkeep is always maintenance regardless of who pays
questions.rationale: Maintenance 'is a routine service done to your asset that typically comes out of own pocket.' Warranty 'is a guarantee to fix your asset if some great damage happens OR if asset doesn't run up to its full potential.' The owner-paid routine service = maintenance. The manufacturer-covered defect fix = warranty.

questions.question: The lecture notes state that 'an asset is often a configuration item BUT configuration items are not necessarily assets.' Which scenario best illustrates the second half of this statement -- a CI that is NOT an asset?
options: A $50,000 server that is tracked in the CMDB
A business-critical database that costs money to run
A software configuration file (e.g., nginx.conf) that has no standalone monetary value but must be tracked for service delivery
A software licence that the company paid for
questions.rationale: An asset has 'intrinsic value to a person or enterprise.' A CI is 'an entity or thing that tracking (monitoring) is required by you for the delivery of a service.' A configuration file like nginx.conf has no monetary value on its own (not an asset) but must be tracked because changes to it affect service delivery -- making it a CI but not an asset.

questions.question: The CI eligibility criterion 'Maintainability' asks a specific question. In which scenario is this criterion MOST relevant to a CI decision?
options: A wall painting in the CEO's office
A web application that requires regular security patching and updates
A one-time batch report that will never be modified
An employee's personal phone not used for work
questions.rationale: The Maintainability criterion asks: 'Are we required to maintain the CI candidate?' A web application requiring regular security patching and updates directly triggers this criterion -- the team must maintain it, making it a strong CI candidate. A wall painting, a one-time report, or a personal phone that requires no IT maintenance would not satisfy this criterion.

questions.question: According to the lecture notes, the CI eligibility criterion 'Delivery cost and quality' asks which question?
options: Is the CI candidate required to help us meet our service commitments?
Is there a monetary cost associated with how the CI is delivered and maintained?
Are we required to track changes made to the CI candidate?
Is the CI candidate crucial to maintaining compliance with standards?
questions.rationale: The Delivery cost and quality criterion asks: 'Is there a monetary cost associated with how the CI is delivered and maintained?' This criterion considers whether the delivery and ongoing maintenance of the item has financial implications that justify formal CI tracking.

questions.question: The PRINCE2 project management framework is described in a single sentence in the lecture notes. Where is it primarily used?
options: Primarily in the United States for large government contracts
Primarily in the UK and Europe for organising projects
Globally for managing IT services and SLAs
Exclusively in military and defence organisations
questions.rationale: The lecture notes describe PRINCE2 as 'a structured method used heavily in the UK/Europe for organizing projects.' This contrasts with PMP which focuses on 'the how of running a project' globally, and ITIL which is 'specifically designed for managing IT Services.'

questions.question: A software team is expanding their CMDB. Someone suggests adding 'all support tickets raised in the past year' as CIs. According to the lecture notes' non-CI examples, why would this be incorrect?
options: Support tickets can be CIs if they are assigned to a developer
Support tickets are not CIs -- they have configurable attributes but are not a direct part of the environment; they are information about entities in the environment
Support tickets are not CIs because they do not have any configurable attributes
Support tickets can only be CIs if they have a monetary cost associated
questions.rationale: The lecture notes give 'an incident ticket' as a non-CI example: 'An incident ticket has configurable attributes but is not a direct part of your environment. It is information about other entities (a computer system, for example) that are part of your environment.' Support/incident tickets are records ABOUT environment components, not components themselves.

questions.question: The lecture notes state SCM 'performs systematic control of changes to the identified attributes throughout SDLC.' Why is this ongoing control throughout the SDLC important, rather than just at the end?
options: Because changes are only made at the end of a project, not during development
Because software keeps changing at every stage, and uncontrolled changes could compromise integrity and traceability at any point
Because the SDLC only has two stages -- development and deployment
Because SCM is only relevant during the testing phase
questions.rationale: The lecture notes emphasise: 'When software keeps changing (which it always does), SCM helps keep things organized and controlled.' Changes happen at every SDLC stage -- requirements evolve, code changes, configurations shift. Without systematic control throughout, integrity (system stays correct) and traceability (who changed what when) cannot be maintained.

questions.question: An IT manager evaluates whether to add their company's customer-facing API gateway to the CMDB as a CI. Using the CI eligibility matrix, which combination of criteria would MOST strongly justify including it?
options: It is rarely changed, has no monetary cost, and is managed by a third party
It has business value, would be impacted by change requests, changes must be tracked, is critical for service commitments, and the company manages it directly
It was purchased recently and has a serial number
It is used by the development team for internal testing only
questions.rationale: An API gateway satisfies multiple CI eligibility criteria: Cost/Value (business-critical), Change considerations (impacted by IT changes), Traceability (changes must be tracked), Management of service commitments (required for service delivery), and the 'Others' criterion (company-managed, not third party). Multiple strong criteria make it a clear CI candidate.

questions.question: The lecture notes state that CM is 'part of an overall service asset and configuration management process' in ITILv3. What does this tell us about CM's scope within ITIL?
options: CM is a standalone process unrelated to other ITIL processes
CM is one component within a broader ITIL process that also covers service assets
CM replaces all other ITIL service management processes
CM only applies to software, not to service assets
questions.rationale: ITILv3 defines CM as 'process responsible for maintaining information about configuration items required to deliver an IT service, including their relationships' and notes it is 'part of an overall service asset and configuration management process.' CM does not operate in isolation -- it is integrated within the broader ITIL service asset management framework.

questions.question: A company's new software product is sold to a customer. Six months later, the product fails to perform as promised. The customer invokes the warranty. What is the manufacturer obligated to do, according to the warranty definition in the lecture notes?
options: Provide a full refund regardless of the nature of the failure
Fix the asset because it has not run up to its full potential (not performing as promised)
Replace the product only if a manufacturing defect is proven in court
Offer a discount on the next version of the product
questions.rationale: A warranty is a 'guarantee to fix your asset if some great damage happens OR if asset doesn't run up to its full potential.' The product failing to perform as promised falls under 'not running up to its full potential,' which triggers the warranty obligation to fix the asset.

questions.question: A systems engineer is documenting which items qualify as CIs in their new cloud infrastructure. They list: (1) The installed PostgreSQL database instance on Server-01, (2) The PostgreSQL software package sitting in the vendor's download portal, (3) The API service running on Server-02, (4) A log entry recording a recent connection error. Which two are CIs?
options: Items 1 and 2
Items 1 and 3
Items 2 and 4
Items 3 and 4
questions.rationale: Item 1 (installed PostgreSQL instance) is a CI -- a logical entity that is a direct part of the environment with configurable attributes. Item 3 (API service running on Server-02) is a CI -- a logical/conceptual entity in the environment with configurable attributes. Item 2 (software package in a vendor portal) is NOT a CI -- only installed instances are CIs; packages are stored in the DML. Item 4 (a log entry/event) is NOT a CI -- events lack configurable attributes and are not direct environment entities.

questions.question: SCM controls changes 'systematically' according to the lecture notes. How does this systematic approach differ from informally managing changes through emails and verbal agreements?
options: Systematic control is slower and less efficient than informal methods
Systematic control ensures integrity (system stays correct) and traceability (every change recorded), preventing uncontrolled modifications that could introduce errors or make issues untraceable
Systematic control only matters for large teams with over 100 developers
There is no practical difference -- both approaches achieve the same result
questions.rationale: SCM 'controls changes systematically. It ensures: Integrity: system remains correct and consistent; Traceability: can track every change (who what when).' Without systematic control, changes might be made without approval (compromising integrity) and without records (losing traceability), making it impossible to determine what changed, when, and why.

questions.question: An organisation debates between PMP, PRINCE2, and ITIL for managing their cloud services team. Which framework should they choose, and why?
options: PMP -- because it is used globally and covers all industries
PRINCE2 -- because it is structured and widely recognised
ITIL -- because it is specifically designed for managing IT Services
Any of the three -- they are all equivalent for IT service management
questions.rationale: ITIL is 'the most relevant one here. It is specifically designed for managing IT Services.' PMP 'focuses on the how of running a project' and PRINCE2 is for 'organizing projects.' For managing an ongoing cloud services operation (not a time-limited project), ITIL's service management focus makes it the most appropriate choice.

questions.question: A CI eligibility matrix lists the criterion 'Others' which includes two sub-questions beyond the main criteria. What are these two additional questions?
options: Is the CI unique? and Is the CI more than 2 years old?
Do we (and not a third party) manage the CI candidate? and Is the CI candidate unique?
Does the CI have a warranty? and Is the CI covered by insurance?
Is the CI a physical item? and Is the CI currently in use?
questions.rationale: The 'Others' criteria in the CI eligibility matrix include: 'Do we, and not a third party manage the CI candidate' and 'Is the CI candidate unique' and 'Other factors specific to your business needs.' Third-party managed items may not belong in your CMDB; uniqueness affects whether a generic vs specific instance should be tracked.

questions.question: The lecture notes define configuration management as ensuring consistency 'throughout its life.' In the context of software, what does 'its life' refer to?
options: Only the initial development phase before the first release
The entire software lifecycle -- from requirements through development, testing, deployment, and maintenance
Only the operational period after the software is deployed to production
The warranty period specified in the software licence agreement
questions.rationale: CM is defined as the 'process for establishing consistency of a product... throughout its life.' SCM specifically 'performs systematic control of changes to the identified attributes throughout SDLC.' This means CM spans the entire lifecycle: from the initial design through development, testing, deployment, and ongoing maintenance -- not just one phase.

questions.question: A team conducts a mid-project SCM review. They find that a new authentication module was added without going through the change approval process. Which SCM activity was bypassed, and which SCM property was put at risk?
options: Configuration audits was bypassed; Traceability was put at risk
Configuration control was bypassed; both Integrity and Traceability were put at risk
Configuration identification was bypassed; only Integrity was put at risk
Configuration status accounting was bypassed; only Traceability was put at risk
questions.rationale: Configuration control 'manages and approves changes' -- bypassing it means the change was not approved. This risks both: Integrity ('system remains correct and consistent' -- an unapproved module could introduce errors) and Traceability ('can track every change who what when' -- the change has no formal record).

questions.question: A company tracks three items in their CMDB: their customer-facing website, the Linux server hosting it, and the SSL certificate securing HTTPS connections. A colleague suggests also adding the IT team's internal Wiki page about the website's architecture. Should the Wiki page be a CI?
options: Yes -- it contains important technical information about a CI
No -- the Wiki page is documentation about the environment, not a direct part of it; it is information about a CI, not a CI itself
Yes -- any documented item qualifies as a CI
No -- only hardware items can be CIs in a CMDB
questions.rationale: A CI must be 'a direct part of our environment, rather than information about such a part.' The website, server, and SSL certificate are direct environment components. The Wiki page is documentation about those components -- similar to how an incident ticket 'is information about other entities that are part of your environment.' Documentation is not itself a CI.

questions.question: The 'Governance and compliance requirements' CI criterion is highly relevant for regulated industries. In which of the following scenarios would this criterion most strongly justify CI classification?
options: A developer's local test environment used only for experimentation
A patient data encryption system that must comply with HIPAA regulations and be auditable
A company intranet used for sharing lunch menus
A deprecated system that is scheduled for decommissioning next month
questions.rationale: The Governance and compliance requirements criterion asks: 'Is the CI candidate crucial to maintaining compliance with standards and other requirements?' A patient data encryption system that must comply with HIPAA and be auditable is directly required for regulatory compliance -- strongly satisfying this criterion. Regulatory requirements often mandate that such systems be formally tracked and managed.

questions.question: SCM identifies both 'functional' and 'physical' attributes of software. Which option correctly gives an example of each type for a database server?
options: Functional: the server's IP address; Physical: the queries it can process per second
Functional: the database's ability to handle 10,000 concurrent connections; Physical: the server's serial number, processor speed, and RAM
Both are the same for software -- there is no physical attribute for a database
Functional: the server room's location; Physical: the database schema version
questions.rationale: SCM 'identifies functional and physical attributes of software at various points in time.' Functional attributes describe what a component does (e.g., 'handles 10,000 concurrent connections'). Physical attributes describe what it physically is (e.g., serial number, processor speed, RAM). Both types are tracked to maintain full configuration consistency.

questions.question: A company is evaluating four candidate items for their CMDB. Item A: a production web server they own and manage. Item B: a vendor-managed SaaS payroll system. Item C: a specific installed instance of their firewall software. Item D: a firewall software package from a vendor's website (not yet installed). Which items should most clearly be CIs?
options: All four -- every IT-related item should be in the CMDB
Items A and C only -- the vendor-managed system and uninstalled package are not direct parts of their environment that they control
Items B and D -- external items need more tracking than internal ones
None -- only physical hardware qualifies as a CI
questions.rationale: Item A (owned production server) = CI -- physical entity in their environment with configurable attributes. Item C (installed firewall instance) = CI -- logical entity in their environment. Item B (vendor-managed SaaS) = NOT a strong CI candidate -- they do not manage it ('Others' criterion: 'Do we, and not a third party manage the CI?'). Item D (uninstalled package) = NOT a CI -- 'only installed instances are CIs; packages are stored in the DML.'
